decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1859, Titusville, Pennsylvania. Edwin Drake and his investors must decide whether to continue drilling after nearly two years of failed, expensive attempts, mounting local ridicule (neighbors reportedly called it "Drake's Folly"), and dwindling funds — using an unproven iron pipe casing technique nobody had successfully used before at that depth. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: the technique could fail exactly as previous attempts had, potentially ending the venture and Drake's reputation for good.Player choice: Continue drilling with the iron pipe casing method, or abandon the well and cut losses.Historical resolution: Drake continued. The well struck oil at 69.5 feet on August 27, 1859, launching the modern petroleum industry.

understand.economic_impact: Drake's 1859 well and the subsequent standardization of the 42-gallon barrel in 1866 created the foundational infrastructure and units still used in global oil markets today Greif history feature. The 1973 oil crisis demonstrated how directly oil supply shocks could disrupt entire national economies, visible in the gas station lines that stretched around blocks across the United States NPR.
understand.political_impact: The 1960 founding of OPEC in Baghdad by five oil-producing nations fundamentally shifted global economic power, giving oil-exporting countries new collective leverage over pricing that had previously been controlled largely by Western companies like Standard Oil GeoExpro. The 1973 oil crisis, driven by OPEC's embargo, showed the direct political consequences of that shifted leverage Yale Energy History.
understand.why_it_still_matters: A single well in rural Pennsylvania, producing only 15 to 25 barrels a day at first — barely anything by today's standards — ended up completely reshaping how the entire world moves, heats itself, and manufactures almost everything you own. Oil started out replacing whale oil in lamps, then became the fuel for cars once those were invented, and today it's still in your phone case, your clothes, and the plane you might fly on. The reason oil prices matter on the news is that when the price of oil changes, it changes the price of nearly everything else too, because oil is hidden inside so much of modern life. Zane's story matters because it shows how one small discovery, in one small town, can end up touching literally every part of how billions of people live — even the parts that have nothing to do with cars or gas stations.
